title: Saving NEMO Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Saving NEMO Act of 2025 makes it against the law to catch, bring into the country, send out of the country, or sell certain types of coral reef animals that might disappear forever. Scientists can still collect these animals for research, and some special breeding programs are allowed. People cannot use bombs or poison to catch these reef animals. If someone breaks this law, they could be fined up to twenty-five thousand dollars or even go to jail.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Natural Resources, and in addition to the Committees on Ways and Means, and Foreign Affairs, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for co

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Saving Natural Ecosystems and Marine Organisms Act of twenty twenty five or the Saving NEMO Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Prohibition With Respect to Certain Marine Reef Species
sections.text:
The Secretary of the Interior must identify certain coral reef species as protected species. This includes any marine species that lives on coral reefs and is listed in a special international agreement called Appendix II of the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species. The Secretary can also add other species to this list if taking them, bringing them into the country, or sending them out of the country could seriously harm the species or the coral reef where they live, or if too many of them die when being moved or kept in captivity.

Once a species is on this protected list, it becomes illegal to take them from waters controlled by the United States, bring them into or out of the country, or buy, sell, transport, or possess them if they were taken or imported illegally. It is also illegal to try to do any of these things.

There are some exceptions to these rules. People can still take or trade these species if they have proper permits for scientific research, museum displays, or zoo breeding programs. Species that come from approved breeding programs or fish farms are also allowed. However, none of these exceptions apply if the animals were collected using harmful methods like explosives, poison, or reef dredging.

If someone breaks these rules, they can face serious penalties. Civil fines can be up to twenty five thousand dollars for each violation. People who knowingly break the law can go to jail for up to six months, or up to two years if they are in the business of importing or exporting these species. Government officials can search for and take away any illegally obtained species and the equipment used to catch them.

Regular citizens can also file lawsuits to stop violations of this law. However, they must first give sixty days written notice to the government and the person they want to sue.

This section defines important terms used throughout the law. Marine reef species are sea creatures that live mainly on coral reefs and are collected for aquariums and decorative purposes, but not for food. Destructive collection practices include using explosives, poison, or reef dredging to catch these animals. The law also explains what makes breeding programs, fish farms, and management plans qualified to be exempt from the prohibitions.

This section will start working one year after this law is passed.
